pr.title: Make labels for the pointers in extabindex
pr.author: PsiLupan

summary: Added explicit global labels inside asm/extab.s for extab records at addresses 0x80005528 through 0x800056A8, then changed asm/extabindex.s to reference those labels instead of raw absolute pointer constants. The PR was a data/assembly cleanup that made extabindex entries symbolic and easier to relocate, audit, and cross-reference without changing the underlying table contents.
what_changed: In asm/extab.s, inserted .global declarations and labels before many two-word extab data records, starting after the existing lbl_80005520 entry and continuing through lbl_800056A8.
In asm/extabindex.s, replaced raw .4byte absolute addresses such as 0x80005528, 0x80005530, and 0x800056A8 with corresponding symbolic labels such as lbl_80005528, lbl_80005530, and lbl_800056A8.
Preserved the extabindex table structure: entries still pair an extab pointer, a function symbol such as func_803984F4 or func_803B6BE4, and a size/length word.
No PR body, comments, or review feedback were present in the provided slice; the intent is inferred from the title and diff only.
smart_moves: Converted address literals into labels at their source data locations, rather than only renaming references in extabindex.s; this keeps producer and consumer tables connected symbolically.
Used address-derived lbl_XXXXXXXX names that exactly match the target addresses, minimizing ambiguity in a raw assembly data table where semantic names were not yet available.
Made each referenced extab record globally visible before using it from extabindex.s, avoiding unresolved-symbol issues across assembly files.
Changed the index entries without altering associated function pointers or size words, keeping the patch narrowly scoped to symbolization.
decomp_lessons: When a table stores pointers into another assembly data blob, label the pointed-to records and reference labels instead of encoding absolute addresses as .4byte constants.
For early-stage decompilation, address-based labels are useful when semantic names are unknown; they improve searchability and cross-references while remaining mechanically verifiable.
Exception/unwind metadata tables such as extab and extabindex benefit from explicit labels because table relationships become visible to the assembler and to later reverse-engineering work.
A safe data-table cleanup can be limited to replacing pointer literals with symbols while preserving all numeric payload words and table ordering.
naming_conventions: Used the project’s generic address label style: lbl_80005528, lbl_80005530, ..., lbl_800056A8.
Kept function entries in extabindex.s in existing generic function-symbol style, e.g. func_803984F4, func_803989A0, func_803B6BE4.
Used uppercase hexadecimal digits in labels where present in the address-derived symbol names, e.g. lbl_800055A0 and lbl_800056A8, matching the diff.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replace raw pointer constants in .4byte directives with assembler labels once the target data has a declared symbol.
Add .global before labels that are referenced from another assembly file, as done in asm/extab.s for labels consumed by asm/extabindex.s.
Preserve binary layout by inserting labels between existing .4byte rows; labels and .global directives do not emit data and should not change the section contents.
Use symbolic references in pointer tables to improve relocation correctness and make map/symbol analysis easier.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that migrate extab or extabindex from raw assembly into C or structured data definitions.
Search for remaining raw 0x800055xx or 0x800056xx pointer constants in assembly tables that could be converted to labels.
Look for follow-up symbol renames giving semantic names to extab records or exception metadata entries.
Check whether other pointer tables in asm use raw .4byte addresses where labels could preserve layout while improving cross-references.
